race.name: Peaks Challenge Falls Creek
race.tagline: Three alpine summits. 235 km. 4,000 meters of climbing. Australia's definitive mountain sportive.

terrain_types: Long sustained climbs to ski resort summits
Fast descents through Victorian High Country
vitals.start_time: 5:00 AM from Falls Creek (early start for the full distance)
vitals.registration: Online via bicyclenetwork.com.au. Opens mid-year prior. Cost: AUD $200-250 (~$130-165 USD).
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple feed stations at key points — valley floors and summit approaches. Water, electrolytes, food.
vitals.cutoff_time: Strict time limits at checkpoints. Riders failing to meet intermediate cutoffs are pulled from course.
distance_options: 3 Peaks Challenge: 235 km (146 mi), 4,000m climbing — the full Triple Peak
Peaks Challenge 7 Peaks: shorter options available on specific peaks

climate.description: March in the Victorian Alps means warm-to-hot valley floors (28-35C / 82-95F) and significantly cooler summits (15-22C / 59-72F). The event starts before dawn to maximize cool riding time and beat the afternoon heat in the valleys. Bushfire smoke has been a factor in some years. Generally dry, but alpine thunderstorms are possible in the afternoon.
challenges: Extreme valley heat: 30-35C on exposed roads between climbs
Temperature differential of 15-20C between valley floors and alpine summits
Pre-dawn start means cold first hour before temperatures climb rapidly
Bushfire smoke in bad years — reduced air quality and visibility
UV exposure is intense in Australian summer — sunburn is a genuine risk on a 10+ hour ride

terrain.primary: Paved alpine roads through Victorian High Country
terrain.surface: Well-maintained sealed roads throughout. Australian alpine highways connecting ski resort towns. Wide lanes on main roads, narrower on summit approaches. All tarmac.
features: Mount Hotham (1,861m) — 30 km of sustained climbing, the longest and highest ascent
Tawonga Gap (1,120m) — shorter but punchy climb connecting valleys
Falls Creek (1,780m) — the final summit, climbed on empty legs after 200+ km
Long, fast descents between peaks — Hotham descent into Harrietville is 30+ km

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Australia's premier climbing sportive. Three alpine summits — Hotham, Tawonga, Falls Creek — connected in a single 235 km day through Victoria's High Country.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: length=5 for the 235 km ultra distance. experience=5 because completing the 3 Peaks is THE bucket-list achievement in Australian road cycling. technicality=1 (all sealed roads). altitude=3 (summits at ~1,800m — moderate but noticeable).

logistics.explanation: Falls Creek is remote by international standards — 4.5 hours from Melbourne, which is itself a long-haul flight from anywhere outside Oceania. The drive up through the Victorian High Country is scenic but winding. Accommodation in Falls Creek village is limited and books out months in advance for event weekend. The event organization by Bicycle Network is professional and experienced, but the geographic isolation is the limiting factor.

length.explanation: 235 km is genuinely ultra distance. This is not a century ride with some climbing — it's nearly 150 miles through mountains. The distance alone would be a serious day on flat roads; combined with 4,000m of climbing, it creates a 10-14 hour effort for most riders. The pre-dawn start reflects the reality that you need every hour of daylight. Few single-day cycling events anywhere in the world are this long.

technicality.explanation: All sealed roads throughout. The descents are fast — particularly the 30+ km descent from Hotham into Harrietville — but the roads are wide and well-maintained. This is a fitness test, not a bike-handling test. A standard road bike is the correct tool.

elevation.explanation: 4,000 meters of climbing across three major alpine summits. Mount Hotham alone accounts for roughly 1,300m of that — 30 km of sustained climbing to 1,861m. The vertical is distributed across three distinct efforts with valley descents between them, which is both a blessing (recovery) and a curse (you have to rebuild momentum three times). This is serious mountain climbing by any standard.

climate.explanation: The heat is the defining climate challenge. Valley floors between the peaks can hit 35C in March, and you're riding through them in the middle of the day when the sun is at its most intense. Australian UV is no joke — this close to the ozone hole, sunburn happens fast. The contrast with cool summit temperatures creates clothing and hydration challenges. Bushfire smoke in bad years adds a respiratory dimension that European events don't contend with.

altitude.explanation: The summits top out at 1,861m (Hotham) and 1,780m (Falls Creek). This is moderate altitude — not enough to cause acute mountain sickness, but enough to reduce power output by 3-5% for sea-level riders. The altitude effect is compounded by fatigue: climbing to 1,800m at kilometer 200 hits differently than at kilometer 50. Acclimatization isn't strictly necessary, but a few days at altitude beforehand helps.

adventure.explanation: The Victorian High Country is genuinely remote and beautiful — eucalyptus forests, alpine meadows, panoramic views from summit ridgelines. The three-peak structure gives the ride a narrative arc: conquer one mountain, descend into the valley, face the next one. The isolation between peaks — long stretches with minimal habitation — creates a sense of journey that loop courses lack. This feels like an expedition, not a lap.

prestige.explanation: Within Australian cycling, this is the event. Completing the 3 Peaks carries real cachet — it's the local equivalent of finishing La Marmotte or the Maratona. But internationally, the event is largely unknown outside Oceania. It lacks the Tour de France associations, the decades of history, or the global participant base that elevate European cyclosportives.

race_quality.explanation: Bicycle Network, Australia's largest cycling organization, runs the event with professional competence. Timing, marshaling, feed stations, and medical support are all solid. The checkpoint cutoff system keeps riders safe without being unreasonably aggressive. The smaller field (2,000-3,000 vs. European events with 7,000+) means less crowding on climbs and a more personal experience.

experience.explanation: The 3 Peaks is a transformative cycling experience. Starting in darkness, climbing through dawn, battling heat in the valleys, and finishing atop Falls Creek on completely empty legs — this is the kind of day that redefines what you think you're capable of. Mount Hotham's 30 km climb is the physical crux, but the final ascent to Falls Creek at kilometer 220 is the emotional one. The sense of achievement at the finish line is profound.

community.explanation: Strong Australian cycling community engagement, particularly from Melbourne and Victorian riders. The shared suffering across 235 km and 4,000m creates bonds, and the smaller field means you see the same faces repeatedly. Post-ride atmosphere in Falls Creek village is warm but modest — this is a mountain town, not a festival venue. International participation is limited by the geographic isolation.

field_depth.explanation: The front of the field includes Australia's strongest amateur road climbers, and fast times are genuinely impressive given the distance and vertical. But the majority rides for completion — finishing the 3 Peaks is the achievement, not beating anyone else. Competitive depth exists but is concentrated among a relatively small group of elite amateurs.

value.explanation: AUD $200-250 for a 235 km mountain sportive with full support is fair value. The entry fee is comparable to European events of similar scale when adjusted for local purchasing power. For what you get — a genuine alpine ultra challenge with professional organization — the cost is reasonable if not exceptional.

expenses.explanation: Falls Creek is a ski resort, and accommodation carries resort pricing even in summer. Limited options mean prices spike for event weekend — AUD $200-400/night for basic accommodation. The 4.5-hour drive from Melbourne adds fuel and potentially overnight stops en route. International visitors face the additional cost of reaching Melbourne (long-haul flights from Europe/North America). Budget AUD $800-1,500 for the weekend, more for international travelers.

biased_opinion.summary: Peaks Challenge Falls Creek is Australia's answer to the great European mountain cyclosportives, and it holds its own. The format is brutally simple: climb three alpine summits, cover 235 km, gain 4,000 meters. The Victorian High Country provides the backdrop — eucalyptus forests, alpine meadows, panoramic ridgeline views — but the real story is the sheer physical and mental challenge of the day. Mount Hotham's 30 km climb is the physical crux. The final ascent to Falls Creek at kilometer 220, on legs that have been working for 10+ hours, is the emotional one. If you finish, you know exactly what you're made of.
strengths: Ultra distance: 235 km with 4,000m of climbing creates a genuine all-day mountain epic
Three-peak structure gives the ride narrative and purpose — each summit is a milestone
Mount Hotham: 30 km of sustained climbing to 1,861m is a world-class climb
The finish: arriving atop Falls Creek on empty legs after 220+ km is cycling at its most raw
weaknesses: Geographic isolation: Falls Creek is 4.5 hours from Melbourne, which is itself remote from most of the world
Valley heat: 30-35C on exposed roads between climbs is punishing and potentially dangerous
Limited international profile: largely unknown outside Australian cycling circles
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Three mountains. 235 kilometers. One day. If you want to know your limits, the Victorian Alps will show you.

final_verdict.one_liner: Australia's definitive mountain challenge. Three alpine summits. 235 km. 4,000 meters of climbing in a single day.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want a genuine ultra-distance mountain cycling challenge outside Europe. Yes if completing Australia's most respected single-day cycling event is on your list. Skip if you can't commit to training for 10-14 hours in the saddle. Skip if the 4.5-hour drive from Melbourne (or long-haul flight from overseas) is a dealbreaker.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want alpine climbing in Australia with less distance: try the individual 7 Peaks rides. If you want ultra-distance cycling in a different format: try the Indian Pacific Wheel Race. If you want a European mountain sportive: try La Marmotte or the Maratona dles Dolomites.

logistics.airport: Melbourne (MEL) — 4.5 hours drive. No closer commercial airports. Most riders drive up and stay in Falls Creek or nearby Bright/Mount Beauty.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Falls Creek village for proximity to the start/finish. Ski lodges operate in summer at lower rates but availability is limited — book 3-6 months ahead. Bright (1.5 hours from Falls Creek) is the larger town alternative with more options. Mount Beauty (base of the Falls Creek climb) is another option.
logistics.food: Alpine Australian — pub meals, bakeries, and basic supplies in Falls Creek village. More dining options in Bright. Self-catering in a ski lodge is common. Feed stations provide standard cycling nutrition — gels, bananas, sandwiches, electrolytes.
logistics.packet_pickup: Falls Creek village, day before the event. Combined with rider briefing.
logistics.parking: Falls Creek village parking. Limited but manageable for the 2,000-3,000 rider field.

history.origin_story: Created by Bicycle Network to give Australian cyclists a genuine alpine challenge that could stand alongside the European mountain cyclosportives. The route connecting three Victorian alpine ski resorts — Falls Creek, Mount Hotham, and via Tawonga Gap — leverages the best climbing terrain in mainland Australia. The 3 Peaks concept became the defining challenge in Australian road cycling within a few years of launch.
notable_moments: 2012: Inaugural edition establishes the 3 Peaks format
Mount Hotham's 30 km climb becomes recognized as one of Australia's great road climbs
Event grows to 2,000-3,000 riders, becoming Australia's premier climbing sportive
Bushfire years test event resilience and rider toughness
history.reputation: The 3 Peaks. If you've finished it, you belong to a club. Australia's most respected single-day cycling achievement.

course_description.character: A linear epic across the Victorian Alps. The three-peak structure gives the ride a natural narrative: conquer Hotham, survive the valley heat, endure Tawonga, and somehow find the legs for Falls Creek at the end. The terrain alternates between long sustained climbs to cool alpine summits and hot, exposed valley roads where the sun beats down relentlessly. There is almost no flat ground — you are either climbing, descending, or wishing you were descending.

suffering_zones.label: Falls Creek to Hotham
suffering_zones.desc: The day begins in darkness with the first major climb: Mount Hotham. 30 km of sustained climbing from the valley floor to 1,861m — the longest single effort of the day. Gradients average 4-5% with steeper sections near the summit. Dawn breaks during the ascent, and the views from the top across the High Country are the first reward. The descent into Harrietville (30+ km) is fast and requires concentration on cold, stiff legs.

suffering_zones.label: Valley Inferno and Tawonga Gap
suffering_zones.desc: The valley roads between Hotham and the Tawonga Gap climb are where the heat hits hardest. By late morning, temperatures can reach 30-35C on exposed roads with minimal shade. Hydration management becomes critical. Tawonga Gap is shorter but punchy — a sharp climb that tests legs already worn from Hotham and the heat.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Hot valley roads, then Tawonga Gap to 1,120m

suffering_zones.label: The Final Ascent to Falls Creek
suffering_zones.desc: The final climb to Falls Creek begins at kilometer 200+. You've been riding for 10+ hours. Your legs know the mileage. The climb to 1,780m is the psychological crux — not the steepest or longest, but the one that demands the most willpower. The summit finish in Falls Creek village, surrounded by exhausted riders who have shared the same 235 km journey, is the defining moment.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Climb to 1,780m on empty legs, 200+ km into the ride

course_description.signature_challenge: The cumulative weight of 235 km and 4,000m. No single climb is the hardest you'll ever ride, but stacking three alpine summits in a single day — with valley heat between them — creates an event where the whole is devastatingly greater than the sum of its parts.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved alpine roads through Victoria's High Country
guide_variables.race_weather: Hot valleys (30-35C), cooler summits (15-22C), intense UV exposure
race_challenges: 4,000m of climbing across three alpine summits in 235 km
Mount Hotham: 30 km sustained climb to 1,861m
Valley heat between peaks: potentially 35C on exposed roads
Final climb to Falls Creek at kilometer 220 on depleted legs

non_negotiables.requirement: Multiple rides of 180+ km with 3,000+ meters of climbing
non_negotiables.why: 235 km with 4,000m of climbing demands training rides that approach the event's distance and vertical. Weekend back-to-back long rides that accumulate 300+ km are ideal preparation.

non_negotiables.requirement: Heat acclimatization and hydration strategy testing
non_negotiables.why: The valley floors between peaks can reach 35C. Training in heat and developing a tested hydration plan — including electrolyte concentration, intake timing, and volume — is essential. Dehydration on a 10+ hour ride is catastrophic.

non_negotiables.requirement: Full dress rehearsal — 200+ km day with 3,000m+ climbing and nutrition plan
non_negotiables.why: Fueling across 10-14 hours in the saddle requires a plan refined through practice. Caloric intake, stomach tolerance under heat stress, and caffeine timing all need testing at race-like duration and intensity.

citations.title: Peaks Challenge Falls Creek — Bicycle Network
